planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist sand or soil after last frost; the plant establishes quickly and can be planted directly into saline or sandy coastal soils.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; this remarkable halophyte naturally colonizes tidal flats, beach margins, and coastal dunes where few other edibles survive, tolerating periodic saltwater flooding and saline spray. The succulent, bright-green to purplish leaves and stems have a pleasantly salty, crisp flavor — rinse before eating to reduce saltiness — and are excellent raw in salads, briefly stir-fried, or used as a sea-vegetable garnish. The plant forms a dense, prostrate mat that suppresses weeds and helps stabilize sandy soils against erosion. In zone 9–11 it is a reliable perennial that can be harvested throughout the year.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or container plants in spring in full sun to partial shade; this species is more drought tolerant than most roses and accepts poor, rocky soils.

directions: Plant in average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this tough native rose requires far less care than hybrid tea roses, resists most diseases, and thrives in neglected areas, roadsides, and dry meadows where other roses struggle. The single, five-petaled pink flowers in late spring attract native bees, and the persistent red rose hips provide late fall and winter food for bluebirds, mockingbirds, and foxes. Rose hips are edible and nutritious, very high in vitamin C; make into jelly, syrup, or tea after removing seeds. Remove the occasional dead cane in winter but otherwise leave unpruned to preserve the natural, wiggly growth habit.

planting.note: Plant thick stem cuttings 3–4 inches long directly in the ground after last frost; the large cuttings root easily and begin producing harvestable leaves within a few months.

directions: Plant thick stem cuttings directly in fertile, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed; this Mayan staple vegetable grows into a large, attractive shrub in zone 9–11 and produces abundant nutritious leaves year-round once established. The leaves must ALWAYS be cooked for at least 5 minutes before eating — raw leaves contain prussic acid (hydrogen cyanide) that is eliminated by heat — but cooked chaya contains more protein, calcium, and iron than most leafy greens. Prepare like spinach in soups, stews, or sautéed as a side dish. Prune plants back by half after harvest to encourage a new flush of tender young leaves; established plants in frost-free areas can produce for many years.

planting.note: Detach a mature pad, allow the cut end to callus for one week in dry shade, then plant 2–3 inches deep in sandy soil; water sparingly until roots form (about 4–6 weeks).

directions: Allow a pad cutting to callus (dry) for one week before planting in sandy or rocky, sharply drained soil in full sun; overwatering is the most common cause of failure. Young pads (nopales) are harvested when 6–8 inches long and still tender, using tongs and a sharp knife to remove glochids (small barbed spines) by scraping before cooking. The pads are eaten grilled, in tacos, scrambled with eggs, or in soups, with a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture. The pear-shaped red or yellow fruits (tunas) that ripen in late summer are sweet and edible; wear thick gloves when harvesting as glochids penetrate skin on contact. Established plants are extremely drought and heat tolerant and require virtually no care.

planting.note: Plant corms or corm pieces with at least one eye, 4–6 inches deep, after soil warms to 65°F and all frost danger is past. Keeps producing side corms for subsequent harvests.

directions: Plant corms in fertile, loose, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial shade; malanga grows naturally in Caribbean and South American forest edges and is more tolerant of shade than taro. The arrow-shaped leaves are ornamental as well as edible (properly cooked), and the large starchy corms have a nuttier, creamier flavor than taro. All parts must be thoroughly cooked before eating as raw malanga contains calcium oxalate crystals. Harvest corms when leaves begin to yellow and die back, typically 7–9 months after planting; the central corm and surrounding cormels can all be harvested. In zone 9 store unharvested corms with heavy mulch over winter or dig and store indoors above 50°F.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division
